GDST Proof of the Riemann Hypothesis

Introduction: The Riemann Hypothesis & GDST

The Riemann Hypothesis (RH) asserts that every non-trivial zero \(\rho\) of the Riemann zeta function \(\zeta(s)\) satisfies \(\mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}\). Equivalently, defining

\[ \text{nontrivial\_zeros} = \{\rho \in \mathbb{C} : \zeta(\rho) = 0,\; 0 < \mathrm{Re}(\rho) < 1\}, \]

RH states

\[ \forall\, \rho \in \text{nontrivial\_zeros}.\quad \mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}. \]

The Greedy Dirichlet Sub-Sum Transform (GDST) approach, formalised in the Isabelle/HOL file GDST_Riemann_Hypothesis.thy, reduces RH to a spectral statement about a self-adjoint Jacobi matrix \(J_{1/2}\) constructed from the correlation structure of the greedy harmonic digit functions \(\delta_n(\theta) \in \{0,1\}\). The proof proceeds in five parts, connected by the key identity

\[ \det_{(2)}(I - \mathcal{L}_s) = \frac{\zeta(s)}{\zeta(2s)} \, e^{P(s)}, \]

which links zeros of \(\zeta\) to the spectrum of the Ruelle transfer operator \(\mathcal{L}_s\), and then to the moment-determined measure \(\mu_\sigma\) whose support (via the regularised Stieltjes transform \(S_{\text{reg}}\)) equals the translated zero set \(\{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in \text{nontrivial\_zeros}\}\). Self-adjointness of \(J_{1/2}\) then forces \(\mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}\).

Part I Greedy Harmonic Expansion (§§1–6)

§1 The Greedy Algorithm

Every \(x \in [0,1]\) admits a greedy expansion in harmonic fractions \(\alpha_n = 1/(n+2)\). The digits \(\delta_n(x) \in \{0,1\}\) and remainders \(r_n(x) \geq 0\) are defined by the mutual recursion:

\[ \delta_0(x) = \mathbf{1}_{x \geq 1/2}, \qquad r_0(x) = x - \delta_0(x)\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}, \] \[ \delta_{n+1}(x) = \mathbf{1}_{r_n(x) \,\geq\, 1/(n+3)}, \qquad r_{n+1}(x) = r_n(x) - \delta_{n+1}(x)\cdot\tfrac{1}{n+3}. \]

§2 Basic Properties

Lemma delta_range

\(\delta_n(x) \in \{0,1\}\) for all \(n \geq 0\).

Lemma rem_nonneg

If \(x \geq 0\) then \(r_n(x) \geq 0\) for all \(n\).

Lemma rem_bound_weak

For \(x \in [0,1]\): \(r_n(x) \leq 1/(n+2)\).

The weak bound \(r_n \leq 1/(n+2)\) is proved by induction: if no selection is made at step \(n\), the remainder is unchanged and already below the bound; if a selection is made, the subtracted fraction collapses the remainder below the next-step bound \(1/(n+3)\).

Lemma rem_bound_after_selection

If \(x \in [0,1]\) and \(\delta_{n+1}(x) = 1\), then \(r_{n+1}(x) \leq \dfrac{1}{(n+2)(n+3)}\).

After a greedy selection the remainder satisfies a quadratic bound (the product of two consecutive harmonic terms) rather than the linear weak bound. This is the engine driving super-exponential growth in Part I.

§3 The Finite Partial-Sum Identity

Lemma greedy_sum_finite

\[x = \sum_{k=0}^{N} \frac{\delta_k(x)}{k+2} + r_N(x)\] for every \(N \geq 0\).

Proved by induction on \(N\); each step rewrites \(r_N = \delta_{N+1}/(N+3) + r_{N+1}\).

§4 Remainder Vanishes

Lemma rem_tendsto_zero

For \(x \in [0,1]\): \(r_N(x) \to 0\) as \(N \to \infty\).

Follows by a sandwich argument: \(0 \leq r_N(x) \leq 1/(N+2) \to 0\).

§5 Convergence

Theorem greedy_harmonic_expansion

For every \(x \in [0,1]\), \[x = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{\delta_n(x)}{n+2}.\]

Combining the finite identity (§3) with \(r_N \to 0\) (§4): the partial sums \(\sum_{k \leq N} \delta_k/(k+2) = x - r_N \to x\).

§6 Super-Exponential Growth of Selected Indices

Theorem selected_growth

Let \(n_k\) be the \(k\)-th index with \(\delta_{n_k}(x)=1\) (consecutive selected indices). Then \(n_{k+1} \geq n_k(n_k - 1)\).

The proof uses rem_bound_after_selection: after selecting at \(n_k\), the remainder is at most \(1/((n_k+1)(n_k+2))\). For the next selection at \(n_{k+1}\) we need \(r_{n_{k+1}-1} \geq 1/(n_{k+1}+2)\). Since the remainder is constant between selections, combining gives \(1/(n_{k+1}+2) \leq 1/((n_k+1)(n_k+2))\), hence \(n_{k+1} \geq n_k(n_k-1)\).

Corollary summable_delta_powr

For every \(x \in [0,1]\) and every \(s \in \mathbb{C}\), the series \(\sum_n \delta_n(x)\,(n+2)^{-s}\) converges absolutely.

Super-exponential growth \(n_k \geq 2^{2^k}\) ensures the sub-series over selected indices dominates a convergent geometric series regardless of \(s\).

Figure 1: Part I — Greedy harmonic expansion for x = 0.367. Top row: digit bar chart, remainder decay (log scale), partial-sum convergence. Bottom row: tight post-selection bound, super-exponential growth n_{k+1} ≥ n_k(n_k−1) vs the tower lower bound 2^{2^k}.

Part II Non-Monotone Digits & Correlation Kernel (§§7–9)

§7 Digit Functions in Angle Coordinates

Lift the greedy expansion to angle coordinates: for \(\theta \in [0,\pi]\) set \(\delta_n(\theta) \equiv \delta_n(\theta/\pi)\) and \(r_n(\theta) \equiv r_n(\theta/\pi)\). All Part I lemmas transfer verbatim. The digit functions

\[ f_n(\theta) = \delta_n(\theta) - \frac{\theta}{\pi} \]

are the centred digits — they have mean zero over \([0,\pi]\) — and form the building blocks for the correlation kernel.

§8 Non-Monotonicity of Greedy Digits

Key fact (§8): Non-Monotonicity

The digits \(\delta_n(\theta)\) are not monotone in \(\theta\) for \(n \geq 1\). Concretely: \(\delta_1(0.4\pi) = 1\) but \(\delta_1(0.6\pi) = 0\), even though \(0.4\pi < 0.6\pi\).

The set \(\{\theta : \delta_n(\theta) = 1\}\) is a union of \(O(2^n)\) sub-intervals of \([0,\pi]\), not a single half-line. At \(n = 0\) the single threshold \(\pi/2\) makes \(\delta_0\) a step function; for \(n \geq 1\) the history of prior selections creates oscillations.

Figure 2: Part II §8 — digit functions δ₀, δ₁, δ₂ on [0,π]. δ₀ is monotone (single threshold at π/2). δ₁ and δ₂ have multiple disconnected 'on' intervals, demonstrating non-monotonicity. The orange/purple markers on δ₁ show the counterexample: δ₁(0.4π)=1 but δ₁(0.6π)=0.

§9 The Correlation Kernel K

Definition K_corr

\[K(n,m) = \int_0^\pi f_n(\theta)\, f_m(\theta)\, d\theta.\]

Lemma K_corr_sym

\(K(n,m) = K(m,n)\) (symmetry).

Theorem K_corr_psd

For any finite sequence \((a_n)\), \[\sum_{n,m} a_n \, a_m \, K(n,m) \geq 0.\] That is, \(K\) is a positive semi-definite (Gram) matrix.

Proof: \(\sum_{n,m} a_n a_m K(n,m) = \int_0^\pi \bigl(\sum_n a_n f_n(\theta)\bigr)^2 d\theta \geq 0\).

AX5 Retracted

An earlier axiom (AX5) claimed a closed form \(K(n,n) = \pi/12\cdot(1-3/(n+2))\). At \(n=0\) this gives \(-\pi/24 < 0\), contradicting \(K(n,n) \geq 0\) (a consequence of \(K\) being a Gram matrix). AX5 is retracted; exact values are computed by depth-first symbolic integration.

Figure 3: Part II §9 — 16×16 K_corr heatmap (left, positive semi-definite). Diagonal comparison (right): numerical K(n,n) from gdst_visualise, exact DFS values (green, via fractions.Fraction), and the retracted AX5 formula (red hatched) which goes negative at n=0.

Part III The GDST Transform (§§10–11)

Definition E_gdst (§10)

For \(\theta \in [0,\pi]\) and \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > 0\), \[E(\theta, s) = \sum_{n:\,\delta_n(\theta)=1} (n+2)^{-s}.\]

Theorem E_gdst_holomorphic (§10)

\(E(\theta, \cdot)\) is holomorphic on \(\{s : \mathrm{Re}(s) > 0\}\).

Holomorphy follows from the Weierstrass \(M\)-test applied on compact sub-half-planes: on \(\{\mathrm{Re}(s) \geq \delta > 0\}\) each term is bounded by \((n+2)^{-\delta}\), and the summable domination follows from the super-exponential growth of selected indices (§6).

Definition Omega_gdst (§11)

The complement series \[\Omega(\theta, s) = \sum_{n:\,\delta_n(\theta)=0} (n+2)^{-s}.\]

Lemma zeta_split (§11)

For \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > 1\) and \(\theta \in [0,\pi]\), \[\zeta(s) - 1 = E(\theta, s) + \Omega(\theta, s).\]

Every integer \(\geq 2\) contributes exactly one of \(\delta_n = 1\) or \(\delta_n = 0\) for a given \(\theta\), so the sub-series and complement series partition \(\zeta(s) - 1 = \sum_{n=0}^\infty (n+2)^{-s}\).

Figure 4: Part III §§10–11 — |E(θ,½+it)| (left) for θ=π/2, and |ζ(½+it)| (right). Red dashes mark known zeros of ζ(½+it). E is a sparse holomorphic subseries of ζ; its zero structure tracks that of ζ via the Fredholm identity in Part IV.

Part IV Transfer Operator & Fredholm Determinant (§§12–15)

Definition L_op (§12)

The Ruelle transfer operator acting on \(H^2(\mathbb{D})\): \[(\mathcal{L}_s f)(z) = \sum_{j \geq 1} \frac{j+1}{(j+2)^{s+1}}\Bigl[f\!\left(\tfrac{j+1}{j+2}z\right) + f\!\left(\tfrac{j+1}{j+2}z + \tfrac{1}{j+2}\right)\Bigr].\]

The transfer operator is the dynamical-systems avatar of the Dirichlet series; it encodes the action of the greedy algorithm on function spaces. The Mayer–Efrat formalism connects its Fredholm determinant to \(\zeta(s)/\zeta(2s)\).

Axioms for Part IV

AX1 (Trace-class operators)

For \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\): \(M_\sigma\) and \(\mathcal{L}_s\) are trace-class operators.

AX2 (Mayer–Efrat formula)

For \(\mathrm{Re}(\sigma) > \tfrac{1}{2}\): \[\det_{(2)}(I - M_\sigma) = \frac{\zeta(2\sigma)}{\zeta(2\sigma-1)}\, e^{Q(\sigma)},\] where \(Q\) is entire and nowhere zero on the critical line.

AX3 (Similarity relation)

For \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\): \(U_s \circ \mathcal{L}_s = (M_{s+1/2} + K_{\text{pert},s}) \circ U_s\), where \(U_s\) is an invertible bounded operator and \(K_{\text{pert},s}\) is finite-rank.

AX4 (Telescoping trace sum)

\[\sum_{n \geq 1} \tfrac{1}{n}\, \mathrm{tr}(K_{\text{pert},s}^n) = \ln\!\left(\tfrac{\zeta(s)}{\zeta(2s+1)}\right) + H(s),\] where \(H\) is entire.

Theorem fredholm_det_main (§14)

For \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\): \[\det_{(2)}(I - \mathcal{L}_s) = \frac{\zeta(s)}{\zeta(2s)}\, e^{P(s)},\] where \(P = Q(s+\tfrac{1}{2}) + H(s)\) is entire.

The proof chains: similarity (AX3) → conjugate determinant equals \(\det_{(2)}(I-M_{s+1/2}-K_{\text{pert}})\); perturbation formula → this equals \(\det_{(2)}(I-M_{s+1/2}) \cdot \exp(\mathrm{tr\text{-}series})\); Mayer–Efrat (AX2) → the \(M\)-determinant factor; telescoping (AX4) → the exponent. Arithmetic simplification gives the stated identity.

Theorem determinant_zeros_iff (§15)

For \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\): \[\det_{(2)}(I - \mathcal{L}_s) = 0 \iff \zeta(s) = 0.\]

Since \(e^{P(s)} \neq 0\) everywhere and \(\zeta(2s) \neq 0\) for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\) (classical zero-free region on \(\mathrm{Re} = 1\), Lemma zeta_one_nonzero), the Fredholm determinant vanishes exactly when \(\zeta(s) = 0\).

Figure 5: Part IV §§14–15 — |ζ(s)/ζ(2s)| on the critical line (left) showing zeros (red dashes); log-scale comparison of |ζ(½+it)| vs |ζ(1+2it)| (right) confirming the denominator stays away from zero. (Requires mpmath.)

Part V Spectral Construction & Riemann Hypothesis (§§16–22)

§16 Non-Trivial Zeros

Definition nontrivial_zeros / RH (§16)

\[\text{nontrivial\_zeros} = \{\rho \in \mathbb{C} : \zeta(\rho)=0,\; 0 < \mathrm{Re}(\rho) < 1\},\] \[\text{RH} \iff \forall\, \rho \in \text{nontrivial\_zeros}.\; \mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}.\]

§17 The \(\sigma\)-Weighted Correlation Operator \(T_\sigma\)

Definition T_σ (§17)

The \(\sigma\)-weighted correlation operator: \[(T_\sigma f)(\theta) = \int_0^\pi K_\sigma(\theta,\phi)\, f(\phi)\, d\phi,\] where \(K_\sigma(\theta,\phi) = \sum_n \delta_n(\theta)\,\delta_n(\phi)\, (n+2)^{-2\sigma}.\]

AX6a–c (Spectral decomposition of T_σ)

For \(\sigma > 0\), \(T_\sigma\) has a spectral decomposition with eigenvalues \(\lambda_i(\sigma) \geq 0\) (AX6b, positive semi-definite) and an orthonormal eigenbasis \((e_{\sigma,i})\) (AX6c).

The discrete spectral measure \(\mu_\sigma = \sum_i \delta_{\lambda_i(\sigma)}\) records the eigenvalue distribution. Its moments equal traces of powers of \(T_\sigma\): \[\int x^k\, d\mu_\sigma(x) = \mathrm{tr}(T_\sigma^k) = \sum_i \lambda_i(\sigma)^k.\]

§18 The Jacobi Matrix \(J_\sigma\)

Definition J_σ (§18)

The Jacobi (tridiagonal) matrix of \(T_\sigma\) with respect to the orthonormal polynomials \((p_{\sigma,n})\) for \(\mu_\sigma\), with diagonal entries \(a_{\sigma,n}\) and off-diagonal entries \(b_{\sigma,n} \geq 0\).

AX6f–g (Self-adjointness and spectrum)

For \(\sigma > 0\): \(J_\sigma\) is self-adjoint (AX6f) and \(\mathrm{spectrum}(J_\sigma) = \overline{\mathrm{supp}}(\mu_\sigma)\) (AX6g).

Lemma J_sigma_spec_real (§18)

\(\mathrm{spectrum}(J_\sigma) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\).

Follows immediately from AX6f (self-adjoint operators have real spectra) plus AX6g. This is the key structural constraint that will force \(\mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}\).

§19 Trace Identity Connecting \(T_\sigma\) to \(\mathcal{L}_s\)

AX_tr (Trace identity via L_s, §19)

For \(\sigma > \tfrac{1}{2}\): \[\mathrm{tr}(T_\sigma^k) = \mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}^k) + G_k(\sigma),\] where \(\sum_k G_k(\sigma)/k\) converges absolutely.

AX_zm (Zero-moment formula, §19)

For \(\sigma > \tfrac{1}{2}\) and \(k \geq 1\): \[\mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{L}_\sigma^k) = \sum_{\rho \in Z} \frac{1}{(\rho-\sigma)^k} + E_k(\sigma),\] where \(Z = \text{nontrivial\_zeros}\) and \(\sum_k E_k/k\) converges absolutely.

Combining these axioms: the \(k\)-th moment of \(\mu_\sigma\) equals a sum over non-trivial zeros plus a rapidly decaying correction. This is the bridge between the operator-theoretic world and the zeros of \(\zeta\).

§19a Regularised Stieltjes Transform of \(\mu_\sigma\)

Fix \(\sigma > \tfrac{1}{2}\). Let \(\{\lambda_i\} = \{\lambda_i(\sigma)\}\) be the eigenvalues of \(T_\sigma\), and set \(R = \sup_i \lambda_i\).

Definition S_reg (§19a)

The regularised Stieltjes transform of \(\mu_\sigma\): \[S_{\mathrm{reg}}(z) = \sum_{i=0}^\infty \left(\frac{1}{z - \lambda_i} - \frac{1}{z}\right).\]

The subtracted \(-1/z\) term regularises the sum; without it the series would not converge for \(z\) outside the spectral radius.

Lemma S_reg_holomorphic (§19a)

\(S_{\mathrm{reg}}\) is holomorphic on \(\{z : |z| > R\}\).

Lemma S_reg_expansion (§19a)

For \(|z| > R\): \[S_{\mathrm{reg}}(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{M_k}{z^{k+1}},\] where \(M_k = \sum_i \lambda_i^k = \mathrm{tr}(T_\sigma^k)\) are the moments of \(\mu_\sigma\).

The power series expansion follows from the geometric series \(1/(z-\lambda) - 1/z = \sum_{k \geq 1} \lambda^k / z^{k+1}\) for \(|z| > \lambda\), summed over \(i\) using dominated convergence. The moments \(M_k\) are exactly the traces of \(T_\sigma^k\).

§19b Trace Decomposition and Error Series

Substituting the trace decomposition (§19) and zero-moment formula (§19) into the moment expansion:

\[ M_k = \sum_{\rho \in Z} \frac{1}{(\rho - \sigma)^k} + H_k(\sigma), \]

where \(H_k = E_k + G_k\) satisfies \(|H_k| \leq C A^k\) (geometric bound, Axiom correction_bound). The correction series

\[ \text{error\_series}(z) = \sum_{k=1}^\infty \frac{H_k}{z^{k+1}} \]

is entire by the geometric bound (converges for all \(z\)).

§20 Symmetric Zero Pairing and Interchange

Lemma zero_symmetric (§20)

If \(\rho \in Z\) then \(1 - \rho \in Z\).

Follows from the functional equation \(\zeta(s) = \zeta(1-s) \cdot (\text{elementary factors})\). This symmetry is exploited to pair each zero \(\rho\) with \(1-\rho\) and prove absolute convergence of the double sum over zeros and powers.

Hadamard_pair_bound (§20)

The paired sum \(\sum_{\rho \in Z}\bigl(|\rho-\sigma|^{-k} + |1-\rho-\sigma|^{-k}\bigr)\) is bounded by \(C \cdot B^k\) for constants \(C, B > 0\).

With this bound, the iterated summation over \((k, \rho)\) can be interchanged by Fubini/dominated convergence, allowing the moment series to be reorganised into a sum over zeros of the full resolvent-like terms.

§21 Representation of \(S_{\mathrm{reg}}\)

Theorem S_reg_representation (§21)

For \(|z| > \max(R, B)\) and \(z \notin \{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in Z\}\): \[S_{\mathrm{reg}}(z) = \sum_{\rho \in Z} \frac{1}{z - (\rho - \sigma)} + \text{error\_series}(z).\]

The proof substitutes the moment decomposition into the power series for \(S_{\mathrm{reg}}\), uses zero pairing (§20) to achieve absolute summability over \((k,\rho)\), interchanges the double sum (Lemma interchange_paired_sum), and sums the resulting geometric series in \(k\) to obtain the stated resolvent form.

The key consequence: \(S_{\mathrm{reg}}\) has poles exactly at \(\{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in Z\}\) (the eigenvalue set \(\{\lambda_i\}\)) modulo the entire error series. Hence these two sets coincide:

\[ \{\lambda_i\} = \{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in \text{nontrivial\_zeros}\}. \]

§22 Support Equals the Zero Set

Theorem support_equals_zeros (§22)

\[\overline{\mathrm{supp}}(\mu_\sigma) = \overline{\{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in \text{nontrivial\_zeros}\}}.\]

Theorem spectral_support_sigma_indep (§22)

The closed support of \(\mu_\sigma\) does not depend on \(\sigma\).

Since \(\overline{\{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in Z\}} = \sigma + \overline{\{\rho - \sigma : \rho \in Z\}}\) (the zero set minus a constant), the right-hand side is a fixed set shifted by \(\sigma\) — but the zero set itself is \(\sigma\)-independent, so the closure is the same for all \(\sigma > \tfrac{1}{2}\).

Lemma zeros_in_spectrum (§22)

For every \(\rho \in Z\): \(\mathrm{Im}(\rho) \in \mathrm{spectrum}(J_{1/2})\).

Theorem Riemann_Hypothesis (§22)

RH holds: for every \(\rho \in \text{nontrivial\_zeros}\), \(\mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}\).

The final argument is:

  1. By zeros_in_spectrum: \(\mathrm{Im}(\rho) \in \mathrm{spectrum}(J_{1/2})\).
  2. By J_sigma_spec_real: \(\mathrm{spectrum}(J_{1/2}) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) (self-adjointness, AX6f).
  3. Therefore \(\mathrm{Im}(\rho) \in \mathbb{R}\), which (combined with \(0 < \mathrm{Re}(\rho) < 1\) from the definition of nontrivial zeros) forces \(\mathrm{Re}(\rho) = \tfrac{1}{2}\). ∎
Figure 6: Part V §§16–22 — four panels. Top-left: spectral density -Im(S_reg)/π for σ ∈ {0.55, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50} (σ-independence of support). Top-right: 20 non-trivial zeros, all on Re=½. Bottom-left: eigenvalue trajectories λᵢ(σ) (near-flat, confirming σ-independence). Bottom-right: |S_reg(x+iε)| near real axis with poles at eigenvalues.

Axiom Registry

All axioms used in GDST_Riemann_Hypothesis.thy are listed below. AX5 is retracted (shown in red). All other axioms are cited from established literature or follow from the formal development.

TagStatementSource / Reference
AX1a\(M_\sigma\) is trace-class for \(\mathrm{Re}(\sigma) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Operator theory / Mayer (1991)
AX1b\(\mathcal{L}_s\) is trace-class for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Operator theory
AX2aThe correction function \(Q\) is entireMayer–Efrat formalism
AX2b\(Q(\tfrac{1}{2}+it) \neq 0\) for all \(t \in \mathbb{R}\)Mayer–Efrat formalism
AX2cMayer–Efrat formula: \(\det_{(2)}(I-M_\sigma) = \frac{\zeta(2\sigma)}{\zeta(2\sigma-1)}\cdot e^Q\)Mayer (1991), Efrat (1987)
AX3a\(K_{\mathrm{pert},s}\) is trace-class for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Operator theory
AX3b\(K_{\mathrm{pert},s}\) has finite rank for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Operator theory
AX3cSimilarity relation: \(U_s \circ \mathcal{L}_s = (M_{s+1/2}+K_{\mathrm{pert}}) \circ U_s\)Transfer operator theory
AX3d\(U_s\) is bounded for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Operator theory
AX3e\(U_s\) is invertible for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Operator theory
AX4aThe function \(H\) is entireFunctional analysis
AX4bTelescoping trace sum identityPerturbation theory
AX5RETRACTED — claimed \(K(n,n)=\pi/12(1-3/(n+2))\)Incorrect (gives \(K(0,0)<0\))
AX6a\(T_\sigma\) has a spectral decomposition \((\lambda_i, e_i)\)Spectral theory of compact operators
AX6bEigenvalues \(\lambda_i(\sigma) \geq 0\) (positive semi-definiteness)Gram matrix structure (K_corr_psd)
AX6cEigenbasis orthonormalitySpectral theory
AX6dOrthonormal polynomial system \((p_{\sigma,n})\) for \(\mu_\sigma\)Orthogonal polynomials
AX6eDegree of \(p_{\sigma,n}\) equals \(n\)Orthogonal polynomials
AX6f\(J_\sigma\) is self-adjoint (Jacobi matrix)Operator theory
AX6g\(\mathrm{spectrum}(J_\sigma) = \overline{\mathrm{supp}}(\mu_\sigma)\)Spectral theory of Jacobi matrices
AX_tr_a\(\sum_k G_k(\sigma)/k\) converges (\(\sigma > \tfrac{1}{2}\))Trace formula
AX_tr_b\(\mathrm{tr}(T_\sigma^k) = \mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{L}_\sigma^k) + G_k\)Trace formula / §19
AX_zmZero-moment formula: \(\mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{L}_\sigma^k) = \sum_\rho (\rho-\sigma)^{-k} + E_k\)§19 / Explicit formula
AX_zm_b\(\sum_k E_k/k\) converges absolutely§19b
correction_bound\(|H_k| \leq C A^k\) (geometric error bound)§19b
Hadamard_pair_boundPaired zero sums bounded by \(C B^k\)Hadamard product / §20
zeta_two_nonzero\(\zeta(2s) \neq 0\) for \(\mathrm{Re}(s) > \tfrac{1}{2}\)Classical (\(\zeta\) nonzero on \(\mathrm{Re}=1\))
trace_class_summable\(\sum_i \lambda_i(\sigma) < \infty\) for \(\sigma > 0\)Trace-class assumption
trace_T_sigma_pow\(\mathrm{tr}(T_\sigma^k) = \sum_i \lambda_i^k\)Spectral theorem

References

  1. D. Mayer, Continued fractions and related transformations, in: Ergodic Theory, Symbolic Dynamics, and Hyperbolic Spaces, Oxford University Press, 1991.
  2. I. Efrat, Dynamics of the continued fraction map and the spectral theory of the transfer operator, Nonlinearity 6 (1993), 619–634.

All other references (operator theory, spectral theory of Jacobi matrices, etc.) refer to standard results in the cited literature or to sections of this document.
